codeSnapshot.label: dpo.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

# DPO Loss (Rafailov et al., 2023)
def dpo_loss(policy_chosen_logprobs, policy_rejected_logprobs,
            ref_chosen_logprobs, ref_rejected_logprobs, beta=0.1):
    """
    policy_chosen_logprobs:   log π_θ(y_w | x) summed over response tokens
    policy_rejected_logprobs: log π_θ(y_l | x)
    ref_chosen_logprobs:      log π_ref(y_w | x)  [frozen reference]
    ref_rejected_logprobs:    log π_ref(y_l | x)
    beta: KL penalty coefficient
    """
    # Log-ratio: policy / reference for each response
    chosen_ratio  = policy_chosen_logprobs  - ref_chosen_logprobs
    rejected_ratio = policy_rejected_logprobs - ref_rejected_logprobs

    # DPO loss: -log σ(β*(chosen_ratio - rejected_ratio))
    logits = beta * (chosen_ratio - rejected_ratio)
    loss = -F.logsigmoid(logits).mean()
    return loss

# Compute log-probabilities for a sequence
def compute_logprobs(model, input_ids, labels):
    """labels: response token positions; -100 for masked positions."""
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(input_ids).logits  # (B, T, V)
    # Shift: logits[t] predicts token[t+1]
    shift_logits = logits[:, :-1, :]      # (B, T-1, V)
    shift_labels = labels[:, 1:]          # (B, T-1)
    log_probs = F.log_softmax(shift_logits, dim=-1)  # (B, T-1, V)
    # Gather log-probs of actual response tokens
    token_log_probs = log_probs.gather(
        2, shift_labels.unsqueeze(-1)).squeeze(-1)  # (B, T-1)
    # Sum over response tokens (mask -100 positions)
    mask = (shift_labels != -100)
    return (token_log_probs * mask).sum(dim=1)  # (B,)

# Training loop
model_name = 'meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-sft'
policy_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
ref_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
ref_model.eval()  # frozen
for p in ref_model.parameters():
    p.requires_grad_(False)

beta = 0.1
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(policy_model.parameters(), lr=5e-7)

eli3: DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) achieves the same goal as RLHF — making the model prefer human-preferred responses — but without training a separate reward model or using reinforcement learning. It's a clever mathematical trick that collapses the RLHF pipeline into a single supervised training step.

The key insight: the optimal RLHF policy can be expressed analytically. Instead of first training a reward model and then using RL to optimise the policy, DPO directly optimises the policy on the preference data. The reward model is implicitly encoded in the policy-to-reference ratio.

DPO is much simpler than RLHF: no PPO, no reward model, no value model. Just a policy model and a frozen reference model. Training cost is 2× model memory instead of 4×. It's now the most common alignment method for open-source LLMs.

whenWhy.title: Simpler alignment than RLHF with comparable results
whenWhy.body: DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) showed that on TL;DR summarisation, DPO achieves win rates comparable to PPO-based RLHF with significantly simpler implementation. TRL (HuggingFace) provides a DPOTrainer that handles the full DPO pipeline in ~50 lines. Most open-source LLMs (Zephyr, OpenHermes, WizardLM 2) now use DPO instead of PPO.

whenWhy.title: Efficient alignment with limited compute
whenWhy.body: DPO requires only 2 model copies (policy + frozen reference) vs 4 for PPO RLHF (policy + reference + reward model + value model). A 7B DPO run needs ~28GB GPU memory (bf16) vs ~112GB for 7B PPO. This makes DPO feasible on 2–4 consumer GPUs, democratising alignment training for the open-source community.

whenWhy.title: SimPO, IPO, and beyond: DPO variants
whenWhy.body: DPO has spawned many variants: IPO (f-divergence generalisation, avoids overfitting), ORPO (ratio-based without reference model), SimPO (simplified target, no reference model, better performance). The DPO family has largely replaced PPO for community-scale alignment. PPO is still used at frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) where the additional cost of RL is justifiable for quality gains.

deepDiveIntro: DPO is derived from the RLHF objective: maximize expected reward minus a KL penalty that keeps the policy close to a reference model. This objective has a closed-form optimal solution — the optimal policy is the reference policy reweighted by the exponential of the reward divided by beta, normalized by a partition function. Taking the log of this relationship expresses the reward as the log ratio of the optimal policy to the reference, plus a normalization term. Substituting this into the Bradley-Terry preference model loss yields the DPO loss — which can be optimized directly on preference pairs without ever computing an explicit reward. [1]

The DPO loss increases the probability of the chosen response relative to the reference model, and simultaneously decreases the probability of the rejected response. Each term is computed as the log probability of the response under the policy minus the log probability under the frozen reference — measuring how much the policy has diverged from the reference on that response. [1]

deepDive.heading: DPO Loss Formula
deepDive.body: The DPO loss computes log-ratios: for the chosen response, the log probability under the policy minus the log probability under the reference; and the same for the rejected response. The loss is the negative log-sigmoid of beta times the difference between these two log-ratios. Minimizing this loss pushes the policy to assign higher relative probability to the chosen response and lower relative probability to the rejected response. The gradient is weighted by a sigmoid term that upweights hard examples — cases where the model currently assigns similar probability to both responses. [1]

deepDive.heading: β Coefficient
deepDive.body: Beta controls how much the policy is allowed to diverge from the reference model. A large beta keeps the policy conservative and close to the reference. A small beta lets the policy deviate more aggressively. The standard value is beta=0.1 (used by Zephyr, OpenHermes). For models that diverge too far from the reference, increasing beta to 0.5 or more stabilizes training. For models that won't learn fast enough, lowering beta to 0.01 gives more aggressive updates. The optimal beta depends on the task and data quality. [1]

deepDive.heading: Data Requirements
deepDive.body: DPO requires triplets of (prompt, chosen response, rejected response). Good datasets include Anthropic HH-RLHF (161K pairs), UltraFeedback (64K pairs scored by GPT-4), and OpenAI TL;DR comparisons. Data quality matters: the rejected response should be clearly worse, not just marginally so. A large gap between chosen and rejected quality produces a stronger learning signal. Synthetic data pipelines that generate multiple responses and use a stronger model to rank them enable a self-improvement loop. GPT-4-judged datasets consistently outperform human-labelled data for DPO training. [1]

deepDive.heading: DPO vs RLHF: Differences
deepDive.body:
| Aspect | RLHF (PPO) | DPO |
|---|---|---|
| Reward model | Explicit (trained separately) | Implicit (encoded in π_θ/π_ref) |
| Training | RL (PPO loop, slow) | Supervised (like SFT) |
| Memory | 4× model | 2× model |
| Stability | Sensitive to KL coeff | More stable |
| Quality ceiling | Higher (explicit reward) | Slightly lower |
PPO is preferred when: you have a strong reward model, need precise control over KL budget, or are training at frontier scale. DPO is preferred when: you want simplicity, lower compute, and acceptable quality for most tasks. [1]

takeaways: L_DPO = −E[log σ(β·(h_w−h_l))] where h = log(π_θ/π_ref) for each response. [1]
No reward model or RL — directly trains policy on preference pairs. [1]
β=0.1 standard; higher β → stay closer to reference; lower β → more aggressive. [1]
2× model memory vs 4× for PPO RLHF — much more compute-efficient. [1]
Now the default alignment method for open-source LLMs (Zephyr, WizardLM 2). [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the 'chosen drift' problem in DPO and how is it addressed?
edgeCases.a: DPO can decrease log π_θ(y_w|x) (the chosen probability) while still satisfying the loss if it decreases log π_θ(y_l|x) even more. The loss only cares about the ratio h_w − h_l being positive — both can decrease as long as y_l decreases faster. This 'forgetting' of the chosen response is called chosen drift. Empirically: DPO models sometimes produce worse outputs than the SFT baseline on some prompts. Fixes: (1) SFT loss on chosen responses added to DPO loss (ORPO); (2) Reference-free DPO (SimPO) with length-normalised log-probs and a margin γ; (3) IPO (Identity Preference Optimisation) regularises more strongly.

edgeCases.q: How does DPO handle ties and near-ties in preference data?
edgeCases.a: In Bradley-Terry preference data, ties (human says 'both are equal') are common. Standard DPO treats all (chosen, rejected) pairs as strict preference — if the pair is actually a tie, it provides noisy gradient signal that can hurt training. Solutions: (1) Filter out ties from training data. (2) Use soft labels: instead of log σ(β(h_w−h_l)), use p_human·log σ(·) + (1−p_human)·log σ(−·) where p_human is the human preference probability (0.5 for a tie). (3) Use preference confidence as a weighting factor — high-confidence (9/10 annotators agree) pairs get more weight.

sources.label: Rafailov et al. (2023) — Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model arXiv:2305.18290
